What I really want to say is that anyone following this should be concerned: a trader with access to private polling data that others can't obtain was able to influence Polymarket's price movements, and then the Wall Street Journal and CNN packaged this price as the collective wisdom of the market and disseminated it. A good prediction market should aggregate a large amount of information from numerous participants into a clear signal. What happened in 2024 was that one person's exclusive polling data was laundered through Polymarket and re-disseminated as if it represented the consensus of thousands of traders. If a trader can do this with $85 million, think about what those who truly have money and power might do? In February 2026, Israeli authorities indicted at least two people, accusing them of using classified military intelligence to gamble on the Polymarket platform. They placed bets on contracts related to Israeli military operations before the operations were made public, with a potential profit of approximately $100,000. These individuals possessed security clearances and used information unavailable to the public for days to wager on the war. This is the first such case globally, demonstrating that prediction markets are fast enough, liquid enough, and anonymous enough to be used to monetize classified information in real time. The Maduro deal mentioned at the beginning of this article follows a similar pattern. Someone placed bets before the secret military operations took place and won over $400,000. Whoever it was, either had inside information or was one of the luckiest bettors in the history of political gambling. We'll never know. What happens when everyone believes the odds? On the Polymarket platform, the median response time for questions is four days. The average response time is 19 days, but a few long-term trading markets can inflate the average. Most questions on the platform relate to the market movements of the week. This suggests that these markets are not making any meaningful long-term predictions about the future. They are simply pricing in near-term events. Will Friday's vote pass? What will this person say tomorrow? Will Wednesday's numbers be higher or lower than expected? This information is certainly useful. But it's very different from what people mean by prediction markets being "truth machines." The term "truth machine" usually implies that the market can tell you what the world will look like six months, a year, or even five years from now. But data shows that it simply cannot. Not at all. 99% of the trading volume in prediction markets is concentrated in the last few hours before an event is settled. Funds flood in during the final hours when the outcome is almost certain. Moreover, these markets have even larger liquidity gaps. By the end of 2025, the combined weekly trading volume of the two major platforms, Polymarket and Kalshi, is projected to reach approximately $2.5 billion. Sounds impressive, right? But the daily clearing volume in the US options market alone is a staggering $760 billion. Prediction markets account for only 0.05% of that. The entire prediction market industry, regardless of the platform, contract, or category, is negligible compared to the markets institutions actually use for decision-making. Here's the thing: prediction markets are only suitable for a very specific type of problem: binary, high-profile, short-term events involving millions of dollars. But this is only a small fraction of the many problems these platforms actually offer. For the remaining 98% of problems, prices are unreliable, liquidity is virtually zero, and the results are more like Twitter polls than financial instruments. They are building the default source of probability for everything. Just as you open the Bloomberg terminal when you want to check stock prices, their vision is that you open Polymarket when you want to check probabilities. Their strategy is that once enough media outlets, newsrooms, financial analysts, and government researchers rely on this data source, regardless of its accuracy, the product will become irreplaceable. I think this will work. And I think it should worry everyone. Because the question isn't whether prediction markets are useful. The answer is yes. For elections, major economic data, and a few high-profile events, they consistently outperform other alternatives. This is a fact, and crucially so. The question is, what happens even for the 98% of questions that are completely unpredictable by the market, when the entire information ecosystem begins to treat these market outputs as truth? Economist Robin Hansen has advocated for prediction markets for decades, describing them as a system that forces people to invest in their beliefs. In his model, the final price will be the best estimate of the current truth. But this model assumes that the market is highly liquid, with diverse participants and is not easily manipulated. Our current market is dominated by a few "whales," concentrated in two areas (elections and sports), accounting for about 80% of total trading volume. The remaining 20% ​​is spread across tens of thousands of contracts, where a few thousand dollars can cause double-digit price fluctuations. These are tools for generating attention. They work when the world is watching; they fail when no one is watching. The more people believe they are tools for creating truth, the more power those who can manipulate prices wield. And those who can manipulate prices are not a group of well-informed ordinary people, but a small group of wealthy traders who control private polls and, in at least two confirmed cases, have access to classified information. The most dangerous thing about prediction markets is not that they can be wrong, but that they consistently predict precisely on key issues, thereby gaining a trust they didn't originally possess. And this trust is gradually being integrated into the world's information processing mechanisms. The Wall Street Journal published the predicted data, CNN broadcast related content, and TikTok also shared the data. Ultimately, a trader with sufficient funds will decide the meaning of this number. This is the reality of the "truth machine." A system that generates numbers, and the world decides to call them truth.

data.title: How is the bottom of a bear market formed? Where is the market now?
data.description:
Author: Murphy Source: X, @Murphychen888

In the past few cycles, observing the relationship between cost base and price behavior has been one of the best perspectives for observing BTC emerging from a bottoming structure.

The logic is that when the price enters the cost range of a certain group of BTC holders, the price movement reflects the behavior of that group. Is it "running away first" or "continuing to hold"?

If the former, the price will encounter resistance near the cost line; if the latter, the price can smoothly break through the cost resistance. If the price fluctuates around the cost line, it means that the market is repeatedly struggling and playing a game.

... Figure 1: Average Cost Line for BTC Holders of 1-3 Months Based on long-term data research experience, I believe that among various groups, the cost basis (1-3m_RP) for short-term holders holding for 1-3 months is the most effective reference (as shown by the yellow line in Figure 1). As can be seen from the figure, in all past bear market cycles, 1-3m_RP has been a key resistance level for rebounds during downtrends. This is because holders of 1-3 months are not very determined. A large portion of them did not intend to hold for the long term when they entered the market. They may have originally only wanted a quick profit, but unexpectedly got trapped and were forced to hold for 1-3 months. When prices rebound and offer an opportunity to exit their positions, they will sell without hesitation. Therefore, we see that in 2015, 2018, and 2022, every time BTC rebounded to the yellow line and encountered resistance, it would continue to pull back; then it would rebound again, encounter resistance again, and pull back again… Of course, many of these were false breakouts, indicating that when some investors started to profit, they “sold off first,” which then led more investors to follow suit, thus creating a false breakout. Ultimately, this reflects investors’ lack of confidence in the market. Figure 2: BTC Supply Held for 1-3 Months For example, looking at the changes in BTC supply held for 1-3 months in Figure 2, there is a clear downward trend after March 29th. This indicates that this portion of the supply has decreased. There are two possibilities for this decrease: 1. Because they continue to hold, they have been reclassified into a longer-term holding group. 2. Because they have sold, they have been reclassified into a shorter-term holding group. Figure 3: Supply of BTC held for 3-6 months. Looking at Figure 3 again, the supply of BTC held for 3-6 months did not increase significantly after March 29th. This proves scenario 2: BTC held for 1-3 months was sold during the rebound, and there are currently 1.09 million coins remaining. Now that the logic is explained, let's return to the present: As of April 15th, the 1-3m_RP is roughly around $75,400, and the price of BTC is also around this level. This is the second time in this downward trend that BTC has rebounded close to this resistance level. The last time was between January 13th and 19th; a slight breakout triggered selling pressure, leading to a continued pullback. Will this happen again this time? Based on past data, I think it's highly likely; after all, in the past three cycles, there has never been a case of a successful reversal on the second attempt. Of course, from a rational perspective, we cannot make "path predictions." Objectively speaking, another possibility is also possible: BTC could break through the yellow line, but then encounter a larger resistance level above, namely the STH-RP (Short-Term Holders Average Cost Line), currently around $81,000, with 2.31 million BTC (far exceeding the 1-3 million BTC holdings). If it encounters resistance here, then BTC may fluctuate around the yellow line, meaning the market needs time to digest the selling pressure and begin to carefully choose a direction. As time goes on, the yellow line will gradually begin to turn upwards, similar to the position marked by the green dotted line in Figure 1. This signifies that the market has completed its bottoming process and entered a "bear-to-bull transition period." Statistically, scenario 1 (the yellow line encountering resistance for the second time) is more likely, but scenario 2 (oscillating around the yellow line) is not entirely impossible. Therefore, we need to observe patiently, and even if there is a temporary breakout, we must determine whether it is genuine or not. In any case, the yellow line is currently trending downwards and is unlikely to suddenly turn upwards. This requires a relatively long transition process, and this process provides us with the best time to make decisions.
